preFix.title: Pig worming and parasite control
preFix.subtitle: When to worm, which products to use, and how to dose accurately for the weight of your pigs.
preFix.excerpt: Outdoor pigs accumulate worm burdens from rooting in previously grazed ground, and indoor pigs can carry parasites from bedding and contact with other animals. Worming is routine management for most smallholding pigs: the timing, product choice, and dosing by estimated weight are the practical details this tutorial covers.
preFix.sourceNotes: AHDB Pork: Health and Welfare on Outdoor Pig Units (2021). DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Pigs (2003, updated 2017). Pig Veterinary Society technical notes.

content.text: Worm burdens in pigs reduce feed conversion, slow growth, and cause liver damage visible at slaughter as milk spots. The main culprit is

, the large roundworm, but outdoor pigs on previously grazed ground may also carry whipworm, nodular worm, and red stomach worm. For most smallholders with a small group of weaners-to-bacon, one or two routine treatments between arrival and slaughter is all that is needed.
Weaners on arrival: treat within the first week of settling in, regardless of the source farm's protocol. This is a standard biosecurity step.
Mid-arc treatment: for pigs on a 6-month weaner-to-bacon arc, treat again at around 3-4 months.
Breeding sows: treat 10-14 days before farrowing to protect the piglets from early contamination.

are prescription-only in the UK. Your vet will supply the product and the dispensing label with the dose. Do not use sheep or cattle wormers on pigs without veterinary guidance; the dose rate and withdrawal periods differ. Fenbendazole granules mixed into feed is the most straightforward method for most smallholders; it requires no restraint and is well-tolerated.
All wormers dose by bodyweight. If you don't have a weigh scale, use the tape-measure method (

) to get an estimate. Always dose to the weight of the heaviest pig in the group if you are treating them together in a feed trough. Underdosing the heaviest pig is the route to treatment failure and resistance.

on the dispensing label and work out the earliest slaughter date before booking the abattoir slot. Fenbendazole products for pigs typically carry a withdrawal of 8-14 days; ivermectin injections may be longer. Record the treatment date and product in your medicine record book as required by law.

Every medicine administered to pigs must be recorded in the pig medicine book: date, product name, batch number, dose, route of administration, withdrawal period, and the pigs treated. Keep records for at least 5 years. The vet's dispensing label provides most of this information.
